user_prompt: implement in `lib\AudioManager\SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp` and `lib\AudioManager\SpeakerPeakActivity.h` a peak level tracker that works exactly like the one used for mic peak level in `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.cpp`, have it analyze samples as they arrive from the bluetooth callback so that they are raw when they are analyzed
agent_reply: - It mirrors the mic peak behavior: signed 16-bit PCM absolute peak, decay over time, `rawPeak()` and `rawPeakLevel()`.
- `AudioManager::hfp_incoming_audio()` now analyzes incoming Bluetooth PCM before it enters the FIFOs, so it is still raw callback audio.
- Added `AudioManager::speakerPeakLevel()` next to the existing mic peak getters.
Build passed. `src/version.c` was regenerated by the build.

commit_message: another pass for neatening the code

user_prompt: ok cool we got 16000 both ways

I got a bug, so when the bluetooth connection is active but the phone is silent, I get no incoming samples, which is fine, except the decay mechanism for tracking speaker peak levels stops decaying it
right now `decay_peak` only operates on `g_raw_peak` anyways so just remove the calling arg for it, rename it `decay_peak_internal`
have the `decay_peak_internal` remember when it did a decay
the public `decay_peak` only does a decay every 20 ms or more, calculating the equivalent `sampleCount` for the given time that has passed without a real `decay_peak_internal` call
call the `decay_peak` from the higher up pump function
agent_reply: Implemented the speaker peak idle decay.

- [SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp): renamed the old internal decay to `decay_peak_internal(size_t frames)`, removed the unused peak argument, and made it remember the last decay time.
- [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp): `pump_bt2spk()` now calls `SpeakerPeakActivity::decay_peak()` before its early returns.
The public decay only runs every `20 ms` or more, converts elapsed silence time into equivalent `16 kHz` sample frames, then applies the same decay slope as real PCM processing.
Implemented the speaker peak idle decay.

- [SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp): renamed the old internal decay to `decay_peak_internal(size_t frames)`, removed the unused peak argument, and made it remember the last decay time.
- [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp): `pump_bt2spk()` now calls `SpeakerPeakActivity::decay_peak()` before its early returns.
The public decay only runs every `20 ms` or more, converts elapsed silence time into equivalent `16 kHz` sample frames, then applies the same decay slope as real PCM processing.

commit_message: diagnostics, fixed a bug with speaker peak decay

user_prompt: calling `tools\code-style-format.ps1`  blows up my github desktop list of changes with a ton of changes but no content changes, is it just line endings?
agent_reply: - `.gitattributes` forces `* text=auto eol=crlf`, so some LF/CRLF noise is real. Git warned specifically about:

So the smaller files are mostly whitespace/formatting noise, but `src/sprites.cpp` is genuine huge churn from `clang-format`. The formatter script runs over all `.c/.cpp/.h/.hpp` under `src`, `include`, `lib`, and `test`, and `src/sprites.cpp` is generated/enormous, so clang-format rewrites a mountain of it.
`src/version.c` is just the generated version hash changing from the build.

Short version: GitHub Desktop’s “tons of changes” is partly line-ending/whitespace, but the big offender is `src/sprites.cpp` being formatted. That file probably wants to be excluded from `tools\code-style-format.ps1`, same as the third-party ignored dirs.

user_prompt: how do I tune `lib\AudioManager\SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp` do decay 4x faster?
agent_reply: In [SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp:116), the decay amount is set here:
That `* 2U / 3U` factor means it decays by about two-thirds of full scale per second. To make it decay **4x faster**, multiply the numerator by 4:
That will make both normal audio-driven decay via `process()` and idle decay via `decay_peak()` fall 4x faster, because both paths call `decay_peak_internal()`.
In [SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp:116), the decay amount is set here:
That `* 2U / 3U` factor means it decays by about two-thirds of full scale per second. To make it decay **4x faster**, multiply the numerator by 4:

user_prompt: place that 2U on top of the file as a rate multiplier
agent_reply: Done. I moved the decay `2` into a top-level constant:
The decay formula now uses `kDecayRateMultiplier`, so making decay 4x faster from the current behavior is just changing that value from `2` to `8`.

user_prompt: when the peaking levels are displayed on the LCD screen, is it linear or logarithmic?
agent_reply: - The level becomes meter width here in [AudioDeviceButton.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/AudioDeviceButton.cpp:104):
The peak-to-percent conversion is linear in both places:
So a displayed `50%` means about half-scale PCM peak, not `-6 dBFS` rendered through a dB meter scale.
